coredata.dc:title: Development of a “Turn-off” fluorescent sensor for acetone from rice straw-derived carbon dots immobilized onto textile cotton mask
coredata.dc:creator: Mogharbel, Amal T.
Pashameah, Rami A.
Alluhaybi, Ahmad A.
Almahri, Albandary
Abumelha, Hana M.
Habeebullah, Turki M.
El-Metwaly, Nashwa M.
coredata.dc:description: Chronic breath can be cited as a warning indication of diabetes-related health problems. Diabetic ketoacidosis can cause a person's breath to smell strongly like acetone, which indicates a high quantity of ketones in blood. So, it has been significant to develop a simple fluorescent textile mask to detect acetone vapor in breath of diabetic patients. On the other hand, rice straw has long been a well-known worldwide solid pollutant. As a result, developing a straightforward process to transform rice straw into a useful product is critical. Herein, a simple and pollution-free approach was devised to reprocess rice straw toward the preparation and immobilization of luminous nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) onto textile cotton mask for detection of acetone. One-pot hydrothermal carbonization of cellulose diacetate with NH4OH as a passivating agent was used to prepare NCDs. The impregnation method was used to place a thin coating of luminous cellulose carbon dots onto cotton fabric. With an emission wavelength of 439 nm and an excitation wavelength of 381 nm, NCDs showed a quantum yield (QY) of up to 24.03 %. Acetone gas was clearly able to quench NCDs' fluorescence resulting from the inner filter effect, demonstrating excellent sensitivity and selectivity. A readout limit of 10 ppb was achieved in an acetone aqueous solution at room temperature, and a linear relationship was found for acetone concentrations of 10–200 ppb.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rice straw
Cotton
Fluorescent
Acetone sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2022R22), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantum yields of NCDs as a function of NH4OH/cellulose diacetate ratio.
TEM images of nitrogen-doped carbon dots.
XRD spectral analysis of cellulose diacetate (a), and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (b).
Cotton assay demonstrating a white surface under daylight (a), and bluish fluorescence under ultraviolet light (b).
SEM images of C6 at different magnifications.
SEM images of C8 at different magnifications.
Fluorescence spectral results of NCDs-cotton assay (C6) in the presence of acetone at various concentrations (10–200 ppb); 200 (a), 150 (b), 100 (c), 50 (d), 10 (e), zero (f) ppb.
Selectivity of NCDs-cotton assay to acetone; the fluorescence spectra upon exposure to metal ions, amino acids, antibiotics and other metabolic volatile compounds (100 ppb).
Excitation spectrum of NCDs-cotton assay C6 (top); and an aqueous solution of CDs(aq) under visible (a) and ultraviolet (b) lights (bottom).
Fatigue resistance of fluorescence intensity at 439 nm (C6).
Coloration properties of cotton fabrics under daytime light (DTL) and ultraviolet light (UV).
Chemical composition of C0, C6 and C8 at three points (a, b and c) of cotton surface.

head.ce:title: Development of a “Turn-off” fluorescent sensor for acetone from rice straw-derived carbon dots immobilized onto textile cotton mask

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chronic breath can be cited as a warning indication of diabetes-related health problems. Diabetic ketoacidosis can cause a person's breath to smell strongly like acetone, which indicates a high quantity of ketones in blood. So, it has been significant to develop a simple fluorescent textile mask to detect acetone vapor in breath of diabetic patients. On the other hand, rice straw has long been a well-known worldwide solid pollutant. As a result, developing a straightforward process to transform rice straw into a useful product is critical. Herein, a simple and pollution-free approach was devised to reprocess rice straw toward the preparation and immobilization of luminous nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) onto textile cotton mask for detection of acetone. One-pot hydrothermal carbonization of cellulose diacetate with NH4OH as a passivating agent was used to prepare NCDs. The impregnation method was used to place a thin coating of luminous cellulose carbon dots onto cotton fabric. With an emission wavelength of 439 nm and an excitation wavelength of 381 nm, NCDs showed a quantum yield (QY) of up to 24.03 %. Acetone gas was clearly able to quench NCDs' fluorescence resulting from the inner filter effect, demonstrating excellent sensitivity and selectivity. A readout limit of 10 ppb was achieved in an acetone aqueous solution at room temperature, and a linear relationship was found for acetone concentrations of 10–200 ppb.

ce:section.ce:para: The predominance of diabetes is expected to rise by 55% between 2013 and 2035, making it a rising worldwide health problem [1]. More than half of people with diabetes are undiagnosed, according to a recent study. Diabetic deaths in nations of low- or middle-income have increased over the last decade, according to data from the World Health Organization (WHO). The development of low-cost, user-friendly, at-home diabetes diagnostic gadgets is an absolute need [2,3]. Solid state gas sensors have great promise for use in breath tests to identify sickness. Medical and environmental diagnostics and monitoring may benefit from the use of ambient temperature gas sensors [4]. There are several advantages to using breathalysers, such as their low cost, portability, ease of distribution, and non-invasive nature. It's possible for diabetics to develop a dangerous disease called diabetic ketoacidosis, in which the acetone in their blood builds up as their liver produces ketones as an alternate energy source for starved cells that are unable to take up glucose [5]. Many researchers have proved such a link between the ketogenic metabolic status and acetone breath. If the acetone gas concentration is more than 1.8 ppm, it can be used to diagnose diabetes in those who are not yet in the crucial ketogenic state. An acetone sensor's detection limit is 50 ppm, which is insufficient for diabetes diagnosis [6]. Acetone detection sensors have recently been developed that are very sensitive. Silicon doped WO3 sensors were developed to function in the range of 320–500 °C and can sense acetone gas as low as 20 ppb [7]. Sub-ppm acetone can be detected using semiconductor sensors, which work best at high temperatures and are susceptible to humidity and reusability issues [8]. The enhanced adsorption of the studied gas by modified semiconductor sensors with bigger dimensions, such as inclusion carbon-nanotubes, has been found to boost sensing [9]. For the detection of acetone, a combination of carbon nanotubes of multi-walled with SnO2 nanoparticles lowered the sensitivity to moisture [10]. In recent decades, researchers have focused on developing breath sensors that are straightforward to use for clinical diagnosis [11]. There are many compounds that can be detected in the breath of a person to indicate the presence of metabolic processes or pathologies, such as H2O2 and NO for asthma, H2 for digestion disorders, acetone for diabetes, ammonia for renal failure, Ethane and pentane for lipid peroxidation, and H2O2 and O2 for respiratory problems [12]. Gas chromatography, mass spectrometry and ion mobility spectrometry have all been utilised to identify specific pathognomic breath biomarkers. Although these approaches need pricey gadgets that require sensitive handling, their potential for developing bedside diagnostic applications is limited because of this limitation [13].

When the analyte comes into touch with a colorimetric gas sensor's receptor, a conversion of the color is immediately visible to the human eye [14]. Forensics, environmental research, analytical chemistry, and biomedical diagnostics have all employed colorimetric sensors. Different chemicals, such as biothiols, metal cations and ammonia, can be detected using fluorescent sensors that demonstrate turn-off, turn-on or turn-on/off behaviour upon exposure to the targeted analyte [15]. Various materials, such as fluorescent metal–organic frameworks, proteins, dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, and carbon dots (CDs) are used in the fabrication of fluorescent sensors [16]. Covalent or physical interactions between fluorescent materials and analyte compounds may occur through hydrogen bonds, coordination, electrostatic interaction, hydrophilic or hydrophobic reactions, and push–pull systems. The spectrophotometric properties of the luminous substance are altered by these interactions [17]. In addition to their biocompatibility, simplicity and tunability, good photostability, and low cost of production, fluorescent carbon dots nanoparticles offer many other advantages [18]. Drug delivery, bioimaging, photocatalysis, optoelectronics, and chemical sensors are all examples of their vast range of applications. They offer an environmental benefit over semiconductors [14]. The strong photostability and unblinking photoluminescence make them preferable to organic fluorophores [19]. A wide range of chemical and inorganic compounds can be detected by fluorescence because CDs exhibit a wide absorption spectrum that extends from ultraviolet to visible range and a broad emission spectrum. Under a UV light, they reveal blue florescence. Polymerization of CDs in a nanocomposite solid form is often more stable as compared to the solution-based polymerization, retaining photoluminescence for up to 17 weeks [20]. Selective sensing of contaminants, biomolecules, biomarkers, metal ions, vitamins, explosives, and medicines can all be accomplished using sensors based on carbon dots [21]. When it comes to monitoring specific ions, carbon dots-based fluorescent sensors are appeared to be highly selective and sensitive, as well as cytotoxic and photostable in aqueous environments for up to 5 weeks [22]. Through the use of a quenching process, carbon dots embedded in sol–gel fibers enable for reversible optical detection of mercury ions [23]. The electron transfer mechanism comprises electron transition from the excited state in CDs to the quenching orbital, may represent the kinetics of fluorescent quenching [20]. Silver ion quenching was the result of a dynamic carbon dot florescent dual-mode sensing tool created for the purpose of selectively detecting silver ions. Increased biocompatibility and selectivity for certain chemical analyte types may be achieved by enriching CDs surface with functional groups [24,25]. CDs-based fluorescence materials enriched with surface functional substituents was developed to detect nitro-containing organic agents. Top-down or bottom-up are straightforward procedures for the syntheses of carbon dot particles [26]. When it comes to the creation of carbon dots, there are several ways to accomplish it, including microwaving, lasers, pyrolysis, carbonization, and hydrothermal methods [13]. A high quantum yield (69%) and superior fluorescence characteristics were shown for carbon dots synthesized from using hydrothermal techniques in bottom-up reactors [27]. There are functional substituents on the surface of CDs that enable them to interact with the analyte species. By inserting carbon dots with inorganic atoms, additional functional substituents are created, and their UV–Vis spectral profiles are shifted, selectivity and quantum yield are increased [28].

In the current study, we created a fluorescent turn-off CDs as a non-invasive quantification detection tool for acetone, which is the chemical hallmark of diabetes. The turn-off fluorescence can be achieved by quenching the acetone interaction efficiency of carbon dots. Fluorescent carbon dots inserted in a cotton fabric were used to identify acetone gas quickly and accurately. Diabetic surveillance relies on the ability to detect acetone gas. There are certain drawbacks to standard detection techniques, such as expensive and time-consuming instruments, as well as lengthy processing times. It is possible to avoid these shortcomings using fluorescence-based detection technologies, which illustrate the advantages of easy manipulation. Aside from their excellent emission properties and inexpensive cost compared to other emissive materials, carbon dots also provide noteworthy benefits in terms of biocompatibility and nontoxicity. A one-pot hydrothermal reaction for producing CDs from rice straw wastes was presented herein in a simple and eco-friendly manner. The present strategy is defined as ecologically benign and cheap large-scale manufacturing that is easy to maintain. The QY was enhanced by using aqueous ammonia as a passivating compound, replacing other expensive and hazardous amine-terminated compounds to introduce nitrogen to the surface of carbon dots. An active “turn on/off” sensor made from these NCDs can be used to detect the gaseous or liquid presence of acetone. Rice straw waste was employed to produce high-quality CDs at a low cost and with little environmental impact. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were employed to examine the produced nanoparticles of NCDs. Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were utilized to report the distribution of carbon spots on the fabric surface. The spectral investigations of absorption and fluorescence were explored. The comfort properties were also examined. The current cotton assay features a “switch-off” fluorescent signal for the detection and measurement of acetone gas. The cellulose carbon dots' ability to quench their own fluorescence in the existence of acetone determines the chromogenic fluorescence of the assay's response.

Cotton fabrics (100%; bleached) were provided from ElMahalla Spinning and Weaving Co., Egypt. Acetone (Ac), gentamicin (GEN), aspartic acid (ASP), threonine (THR), clarithromycin (CLA), lysine (LYS), amoxicillin (AMO), tyrosine (TYR), tryptophan (TRY), valine (VAL), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2; HP), pentane (PNT), malondialdehyde (MDA), ammonium hydroxide, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), tetrabutylammonium acetate (TBAA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetic anhydride, and sodium chlorite (NaClO2), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, Fluka and Merck. All experiments were carried out using distilled water. Rice straw was provided from the farms of Sharqia Governorate, Egypt.

Starting from rice straw, cellulose diacetate was prepared as reported in previous studies [29,30]. Multiple water washes were applied to remove dust and other solid contaminants from the rice straw waste, which was then diced up and dried for an hour at 80 °C to remove any remaining moisture. The rice straws were soaked in a 17.5 % (w/w) NaOH(aq) for two hours. The swelled straws were exposed to rinsing and blending in a blender for ∼ 30 min before drying under ambient conditions. The cellulose amorphous areas of rice straw breakdown intermolecular bonds in the plant cell walls when sodium hydroxide is added. The hydrolysis process was carried out at 80 °C to eliminate any leftover hemicelluloses and cellulose amorphous components from the final product. Following exposure to de-ionized water and drying under ambient conditions, the pH was returned to neutral. After two hours of heating (80 °C) in NaOH(aq) (2%; w/v) with a steady stirring speed, the hydrolyzed product was rinsed and air-dried. Because of the insoluble lignin, it remained brown even after being treated with alkali. It took an hour at 50 °C to apply NaClO2(aq) (20%; w/v) to finish the bleaching step. The sodium chlorite dosage was determined by calculating the kappa number for each fiber. In addition to being bleached, the fibers underwent a thorough cleaning and drying process. One percent of cellulose fiber (w/w) in distilled water required 30 min of homogenization (20 KHz) in a cylindrical probe (1.5 cm) to generate nanofibrillated architectures (125 mL). To keep the temperature stable, the homogenization was done in an ice bath. As reported in previous studies [30], the magnitude of substitution (DS) of cellulose diacetate was monitored at 2.62. The DMSO-TBAA (8:2) solvent mixture was progressively supplemented with rice straw-derived cellulose fibers. At 60 °C, a transparent solution of cellulose was produced after 30 min of stirring. Ethanoic anhydride was poured slowly to the cellulose solution. Methanol precipitation was used after the solution had been stirred for three hours at 60 °C. Finally, cellulose diacetate was produced after filtering and washing with methanol.

The hydrothermal procedure was used to create NCDs. After treatment with aqueous ammonia, cellulose diacetate (0.4 g) was dispersed in distilled water (40 mL). Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) was then employed to heat the solution to 240 °C for 16 hrs. After reaction completion, the autoclave was returned to the ambient temperature. Precipitates were removed by centrifuging the mixture for 15 min at 10000 rpm. Dialysis for 22 h using distilled water in a vial (MWCO1000) after ultrafine filter (0.22 m) purification removed contaminants. The nitrogen-doped CDs were reserved at 4 °C for further studies.

Various amounts of NCDs (zero, 5, 15, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65, and 75 mg) were dispersed in distilled water (100 mL). A buffer solution of Na2HPO4 (200 μM) was added to the provided solutions, which were then applied onto a cotton fabric (10 × 15 cm) by impregnation for 5 min. The fabrics were air-dries for an hour. Depending on the concentration of NCDs in distilled water, the treated cotton fabrics were represented by C0, C1, C2, C3, C4, C5, C6, C7 and C8, respectively. Photographs of CDs-cotton assay were taken under ultraviolet lamp using Canon A710IS.

NCDs-imprinted cotton assay was used to detect acetone in both aqueous (de-ionized) and gaseous states at room temperature. De-ionized water was used to dilute acetone (100 L) at various contents (10–200 ppb). The NCDs-imprinted cotton assay was then exposed to acetone solutions for 5 min, and air-dried for 30 min. Excitation at 381 nm then yielded the emission spectra. In order to examine the cotton sensor mask against acetone vapor, liquid pure acetone (10 mL) was placed in a glass tube (20 mL). The cotton sensor mask was placed close to the top of the glass tube to signify quenching of blue fluorescence. As a means of assessing the selectivity of the NCDs-imprinted cotton assay to acetone, it was impregnated with solutions of different chemicals such as K+, Na+, Ca++, GEN, LYS, CLA, TRY, VAL, AMO, ASP, THR, PNT, HP, and MDA. In order to examine the reversibility of sensor, no variations in the emission intensities of the NCDs-imprinted cotton assay were detected in the presence or absence of gaseous acetone, indicating high reversibility and durability of sensor fabric.

The use of an FP-6500 fluorospectrometer (Tokyo, Japan) allowed for the collection of photoluminescent spectrum data. A UV1006M031 spectrometer (Shanghai; China) was used to measure the absorption spectra of CDs. It was necessary to capture the color data using UltraScan Pro (HunterLab; United States) before and after UV exposure [31]. To measure the color of NCDs-imprinted samples, fabrics were subjected to ultraviolet rays for five minutes. An UV source (365 nm) was used 5 cm above the cotton mask. When the ultraviolet source was removed, the coloration parameters were recorded instantly. Equation (1) was employed to calculate the QY of NCDs at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm using a solution of quinine sulfate standard (0.1 M) in H2SO4 with a quantum yield of 54% [13].
(1)
QY
=
I
/
I
r
∗
A
r
/
A
∗
η
2
/
η
r 2
where r is the standard sample, η is the solvent refractive index, A is the absorbance intensity, and I is the emission intensity.

Nexus 670 (Nicolet, USA) was utilized to obtain the FTIR spectra of NCDs. XRD Studying the morphology and chemical composition of NCDs-imprinted fabric was accomplished using an EDXA and Quanta FEG-250 SEM (Czech Republic). TEM (JEOL1230; Japan) was utilized to investigate NCDs in terms of their shape and size.

The comfort properties were examined by taking measurements of air-permeability and bend length using Textest FX3300 (ASTM D737) [15], and Shirley Stiffness device (British 3356:1961) [32], respectively.

The synthesis of CDs by hydrothermal curing of cellulose diacetate at 240 °C for 15 h is shown in Scheme S1. After being soaked in NaOH(aq), rice straw was hydrolyzed with a solution of diluted HCl(aq). To prepare cellulose diacetate, a solution of DMSO/TBAA was used to treat rice straw waste cellulose fibers, followed by treatment with acetic anhydride. Dilute NH4OH(aq) was used as a passivation reaction medium replacing other costly and dangerous passivation chemicals that usually contains multiple terminal amine groups. As shown in Fig. 1 , the aqueous ammonia to cellulose diacetate mass ratio was studied in order to report the ideal QY of NCDs. The mass ratio of aqueous ammonia to cellulose diacetate was changed from 1:0 to 1:10 to boost QY from 9.57% to 24.03%. Raising the mass ratio to 1:15 would result in a further decline in QY. NCDs with QY as high as 24.03% were made using different mass ratios of ammonium hydroxide and cellulose diacetate, which were in line with earlier studies. Previous studies on cellulose degradation/carbonization have shown that CDs could be prepared by cellulose diacetate hydrolysis and subsequent condensation of small fragments to produce NCDs. The cellulose derivative, known as cellulose diacetate, has glucose-like units attached together by glycoside bonds [30]. In NH4OH(aq), glycoside bond breaking and deacetylation of cellulose diacetate can yield fructose, acetic acid, and other cellulose diacetate intermediate fragments [30]. Researchers believe that NH4OH may have formed novel oxygen and nitrogen group configurations. The previously identified fragments of cellulose diacetate were subjected to condensation/carbonization during hydrothermal treatment [33]. For a successful passivation of nitrogen on the surface of CD, nucleophilic substitution of ammonia to the carbon atom bearing a hydroxyl group on the surface of CD proved crucial [34]. TEM images in Fig. 2 indicate CDs with sizes of 71–101 nm. NCDs do not have a lattice structure, according to prior studies [35]. While the carbon dots only revealed two wide XRD bands at 16.2° and 22.9°, the cellulose diacetate amorphous phase can be shown in its XRD pattern at 7.3° and 20.1°, which are ascribed to the normal amorphous phase of cellulose diacetate ( Fig. 3
). FTIR spectra were used to identify the NCDs surface functional groups. In addition to the stretch vibrations at 3279 cm−1, bending vibrations at 1254 and 783 cm−1 were found to be related with both NH and O–H, respectively. Cellulose diacetate has been successfully nitrogen-doped, as proved by FTIR and XRD spectra. Vibrations of the SO3 and C-O stretches at 1007 cm−1 revealed that sulfur was present in NCDs [33,36].

The NCDs-treated cotton fabrics were tested for their colorimetric properties as depicted in Table 1 . Using the CIE Lab colorimetric screening, the photo-switchable NCDs-treated cotton fabrics were evaluated for their technical performance. The coated film was transparent since both blank and CDs-coated fabrics were white in color. K/S slightly increased in daylight, and considerably increased under UV light as the NCDs ratio increased, indicating that the tinctorial strength had improved. It was found that the K/S was marginally higher under daylight and much higher under UV rays because of the increasing NCDs ratios in daylight. In daytime light, the L*, a* and b* values of CDs-coated cotton fabrics displayed almost no changes as compared to those of blank cotton fabric. No change in K/S and CIE Lab values of the white cotton fabrics in daylight before or after coating was observed due to low content of CDs on fabric surface, confirming transparency of the coated photochromic layer onto the fabric surface. The best colorimetric findings were monitored at a NCDs concentration of 55 mg. The excitation and emission intensities of the CDs-coated film were observed to increase when the CDs-cotton fabrics were exposed to UV light. When the NCD content was raised, there were very little changes in –a* and + b* in daylight, while L* displayed a slight decrease. Both of –a* and –b* rose underneath ultraviolet rays but L* decreased when the cotton fabric was exposed to UV rays, indicating that the fabric was covered with a colorless coating. These results indicate a color shift in these coated cotton fabrics to blue.

Despite its widespread use as a staple meal, rice still creates a significant amount of straw and husk per year (8 × 1011 kg of straws and 1.5 × 1011 kg of husk per year) [37]. For the next harvest, the majority of rice straw waste is either subjected to burning or mulching. Burning rice straw has lost its appeal as a waste disposal option because of the greenhouse gas emissions and the resulting smoke. A 20% of rice straw is now used in the manufacturing of animal feed, biofuel and fertilizers [38]. On the other side, rice straw degrades in soil with time and might be the cause of rice diseases. Efforts were made to find economic and socially viable applications for agricultural waste. It is possible to utilize rice bran as a functional meal due to its ability to reduce Salmonella bacteria in the gastrointestinal tracts. Rice straw is an excellent source of oil for both humans and animals [39]. Rice straw has been considered as a potential source of biofuels and other products. It was formerly thought of as trash and typically burnt, but since the rice husks were manufactured off-site, a higher percentage of this waste has been put to use. For many years, rice straw is utilized for energy production since it is easily accessible and inexpensive. Many rice straw products, such as lumbers and resins, have been developed in recent years by admixing pulverized rice straw with resins [40]. Smart products are capable of adjusting their characteristics in response to external stimuli [41]. Fluorescent sensors are one kind of smart material that changes color when exposed to light. Because of how simple they are to produce and use, as well as their wide range of applications, sensor fabrics have lately been a popular choice for many commercial products. As indicated in Figs. 5-6
, SEM images were taken of a fluorescent NCDs film coated onto a cotton fabric. Using SEM images, we were able to establish that a thin layer of CDs was deposited onto fabric surface. Deposition of NCDs onto the cellulose cotton surface was successful, as shown by SEM images. Since NCDs are so small, they were able to cover the cellulose surface in a uniform layer. SEM images revealed no variations in cotton fibrous structure of treated or untreated fabrics. As shown in Table 2 , the elemental concentration of C6 was determined by using EDX. NCDs-treated fabric had almost identical elemental concentrations in all three sites where they were tested. Compared to cellulose diacetate, the amounts of nitrogen and sulfur in CDs were 9.58% and 1.77%, respectively, indicating that doping is more effective. N-doped CDs were produced using just cellulose diacetate and ammonium hydroxide. Thus, nitrogen was mostly arising from ammonium hydroxide, and cellulose diacetate was observed as the source of carbon.

Because the fluorescence of NCDs can be reversibly exposed to quenching by acetone, a “turn-off” fluorescence sensing tool for acetone was constructed utilizing NCDs-cotton assay. High fluorescence stability was observed in the NCDs-cotton assay (C6) throughout a pH range of 5–7. The fluorescence of NCDs-cotton assay in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of acetone, were used to calculate quenching efficiency (F/F0). As can be seen in Fig. 7 , the quenching efficacy of the NCDs-cotton assay in various acetone solutions was analyzed by emission spectra. The maximum fluorescence intensity of the NCDs-cotton assay dropped when acetone concentration increased from 10 to 200 ppb. The efficacy of quenching was shown to be linearly related to acetone concentrations ranging from 10 to 200 ppb. Furthermore, using the rule of 3, a detection limit of 10 ppb was established. In comparison to previous NCDs-cotton sensors, the new one has a lower detection limit and a wider linear range (10–200 ppb). The current NCDs-cotton assay had comparable or higher accuracy than previously described methods, according to the results. As a means of testing for acetone detection selectively, other compounds that may be present in physiological systems like amino acids (VAL, LYS, TYR, THR and ASP), antibiotics antibiotics (AMO, CLA and GEN), metals (K+, Na+ and Ca++), and other volatile metabolic gaseous compounds (HP, PNT and MDA) were also examined, and their fluorescence spectra and efficiency of quenching were reported ( Fig. 8
). As a result, the quenching effectiveness of the NCDs-cotton assay was estimated by observing that its emission maxima did not vary much when immersed in solutions of the aforesaid competing chemicals. There was an obvious quenching in the acetone solution, as the fluorescence intensity dropped by around 85%. While the NCDs-cotton assay exhibited a lesser response to the bulk of other substances, this suggests that the cotton assay has an unusual preference for acetone as a solvent. Possible NCD quenching mechanisms were investigated. Fig. 2 exhibited the TEM analysis of the acetone-induced aggregation of NCDs. After immersion in the acetone solution, it was observed that NCDs created no larger particles. Following acetone immersion, the acetone-induced reduction in the diameter of NCDs particles was found to be almost the same as the acetone-induced reduction in the diameter of NCDs particles prior to the acetone immersion. There was most likely some kind of adsorption that slowed down NCDs emission. Fig. 9 depicts the results of an excitation spectrum study performed on an aqueous solution of NCDs. Even though the absorbance spectra of acetone and that of NCDs were discovered to overlap to some extent, it was determined that only sections of these spectrums overlapped. The inner filter effect phenomenon could be responsible for quenching emission [13,33]. Experiments were carried out to further demonstrate the IFE effect, and it was found that the NCDs-cotton assay had emission life-times of 7.55 ns and 7.31 ns, before and after acetone exposure, respectively. To establish that there is no fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between acetone and CDs, we determined the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs-cotton assay before and after exposing to acetone.

Results of testing NCDs-cotton assay for fatigue resistance in the presence of acetone and UV irradiation are shown in Fig. 10
. NCDs-cotton assay (C6) was exposed to a source of acetone vapor, and then removed away from the source of exposure. After each acetone cycle, the level of emission was measured and recorded. In the absence and presence of acetone gas, there were no noticeable variations in emission intensities. This indicates a high level of durability and reversibility. Additionally, the NCDs-cotton assay (C6) displayed a good durability as the same emission intensity was determined without changes after four weeks of storage.

Both of air-permeability and Bend length screening results are displayed in Table 3 . No evident differences were detected in air-permeability with raised the CDs ratio. However, the bend-length of the CDs-treated cotton was found to slightly decrease with increasing the CDs ratio.

Nanomaterials derived from rice straw have been produced. Aqueous passivating ammonia was utilized in a one-pot hydrothermal preparation of CDs from rice straw derived cellulose diacetate. NCDs were also examined in terms of how they may have developed. In terms of emission, NCDs stand out, with a QY of up to 24.03%. Examining the deposited luminous security NCDs films on cotton assay was done by a variety of analysis methods. The NCDs-cotton assay exhibited a white look when seen in the daytime. Using ultraviolet light, researchers discovered that acetone quenches the photochromic blue emission. There was no noticeable difference in mechanical properties between the NCDs-cotton assay and blank fabric. This transparency of the coated film was created by dispersing nano-scale carbon dots in an even distribution over the surface. No effective changes were detected in the comfort properties with increasing the CDs ratio. NCDs-cotton assay fluorescence was selective to quenching by acetone, according to the study. A linear relationship was found among the effectiveness of fluorescence quenching and concentration of acetone (10–200 ppb), revealing a detection limit of 10 ppb. The inner filter effect was proposed as the cause of fluorescence quenching. As part of the present plan, rice straw can be converted to valuable nano-sized materials, which can then be used to develop a sensor assay for the detection of acetone, as well as monitoring of diabetes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2022R22), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
